active_paradigms.idea: Use scipy.optimize.linprog for exact radius computation
active_paradigms.description: Replace the iterative radius adjustment with exact linear programming. Given fixed centers, the maximum radii problem has linear constraints (ri + rj ≤ distance_ij, ri ≤ boundary distance) and linear objective (maximize sum ri). Use scipy.optimize.linprog with method='highs' to solve this exactly. This guarantees optimal radii for any given positions, eliminating approximation error from iterative methods. The position optimization can still use differential_evolution, but with exact LP-based radius computation.
active_paradigms.what_to_optimize: Accuracy of radius computation for given positions; eliminates suboptimality from iterative adjustment
active_paradigms.cautions: LP has O(n²) constraints for n circles; use sparse matrices if needed. Handle infeasible cases gracefully. The 'highs' method is the most robust modern LP solver in scipy.

active_paradigms.idea: Use scipy.optimize.fmin_cobyla with direct constraint handling
active_paradigms.description: COBYLA (Constrained Optimization BY Linear Approximation) handles constraints directly without penalty functions or gradients. Define the objective as negative sum of radii, with constraint functions for: (1) centers inside [0.05, 0.95]², (2) minimum clearance between circles. The key advantage is COBYLA builds linear approximations of constraints locally and respects them exactly, unlike penalty-based methods. Use hexagonal-pattern initial guess for faster convergence.
active_paradigms.what_to_optimize: Better handling of the non-smooth constraint boundaries; direct constraint satisfaction without penalty tuning
active_paradigms.cautions: COBYLA requires constraint functions that return positive values when satisfied. The radius computation inside constraints must be fast. Start with conservative bounds [0.1, 0.9] to ensure feasibility.

active_paradigms.description: Powell's conjugate direction method is derivative-free, making it suitable for non-smooth objectives. The key innovation is using a carefully constructed hexagonal-pattern initial guess: place circles in layers following hexagonal close-packing geometry, with edge circles positioned to maximize boundary contact. Powell's method will refine positions along conjugate directions without needing gradient information, potentially escaping local optima that trap gradient-based methods.
active_paradigms.what_to_optimize: Escape local optima through derivative-free search; leverage geometric structure in initialization
active_paradigms.cautions: Powell's method can be slow; set maxiter appropriately. The initial guess quality is critical - use proper hexagonal spacing with spacing parameter ≈ 0.2 for 26 circles. Combine with exact LP radius computation.

tried_paradigms.idea: Parametric hexagonal template with 5-10 parameters instead of 52
tried_paradigms.description: Drastically reduce dimensionality: define a parametric template for the packing (hexagonal grid with row configuration [5,6,5,5,5] or similar). Parameters: horizontal spacing s, vertical spacing s*sqrt(3)/2, x-offset, y-offset, and possibly per-row adjustments. Optimize only these 5-10 parameters instead of 52 individual positions. This makes the optimization problem much simpler and avoids local minima from the high-dimensional space.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: Template parameters that define the entire 26-circle configuration
tried_paradigms.cautions: The template constrains the solution space - may not reach optimal if optimal doesn't match template structure. Use multiple template variants (different row configurations) and pick best result.

tried_paradigms.idea: Use scipy.optimize.dual_annealing for global optimization of circle positions
tried_paradigms.description: Apply dual annealing (simulated annealing combined with local search) to optimize circle positions. This algorithm uses a temperature-based acceptance criterion that allows escaping local optima by accepting worse solutions with decreasing probability. The objective function computes maximum radii for given positions using iterative constraint propagation. Use bounds [0.02, 0.98] for each coordinate to allow circles closer to boundaries, maxiter=2000, initial_temp=5230, and local search with L-BFGS-B for refinement. The annealing schedule explores broadly at high temperatures and exploits at low temperatures.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: Maximize sum of radii by finding optimal circle positions through temperature-based global search
tried_paradigms.cautions: Dual annealing requires careful tuning of initial temperature and annealing schedule. Use restarts option to avoid local optima. The radius computation should be efficient as it's called many times. Consider using no_local_search=False for local refinement after global search. Bounds should allow circles near boundaries for larger radii.

tried_paradigms.idea: Use scipy.optimize.minimize with trust-constr method for joint position-radius optimization
tried_paradigms.description: Jointly optimize positions (52 variables) and radii (26 variables) using the trust-constr method with explicit non-linear constraints. Define constraints for non-overlap (||c_i - c_j|| >= r_i + r_j) and boundaries (r_i <= min(x_i, y_i, 1-x_i, 1-y_i)). Trust-constr uses a trust-region approach with sequential quadratic programming for constraint handling, which is fundamentally different from COBYLA's linear approximation or SLSQP's active-set method. Initialize positions in a hexagonal-like pattern and radii at half the minimum distance to nearest neighbor or boundary.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: Maximize sum of radii with explicit constraint handling through trust-region optimization
tried_paradigms.cautions: Trust-constr requires good initialization to converge. Use sparse Jacobian representation for the 325 pairwise constraints. The constraint Jacobian should be provided analytically for efficiency. Consider using 'lsmr' subproblem solver for better handling of ill-conditioned problems. Use verbose output to monitor convergence. Initial radii should be conservative to satisfy constraints.

tried_paradigms.idea: Use scipy.optimize.differential_evolution with randtobest1exp strategy and adaptive parameters
tried_paradigms.description: Use differential evolution with the 'randtobest1exp' mutation strategy instead of the current 'best1bin'. The randtobest1exp strategy uses a random base vector mutated towards the best solution with exponential crossover, providing different exploration-exploitation balance. This strategy can better escape local optima by incorporating randomness in the mutation process. Increase popsize to 30, maxiter to 1000, use mutation=(0.5, 1.5) for broader search range, and recombination=0.9 for good diversity. Use bounds [0.02, 0.98] to allow circles closer to boundaries.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: Maximize sum of radii through evolutionary optimization with randtobest mutation strategy
tried_paradigms.cautions: The randtobest1exp strategy may require more generations to converge than best1bin. Ensure adequate population diversity with larger popsize. Monitor convergence and consider using polish=True for final local refinement. The mutation range (0.5, 1.5) allows broader exploration than the current (0.5, 1.0). Use seed=42 for reproducibility.

tried_paradigms.idea: Use scipy.optimize.dual_annealing for global optimization with better escape from local optima
tried_paradigms.description: Apply scipy.optimize.dual_annealing to optimize circle positions (52 variables). Dual annealing combines simulated annealing's global search with local optimization, potentially finding better solutions than differential_evolution. Use the same iterative radius computation but with different search dynamics. Set initial_temp high (5230), restart_temp_ratio=2e-5, and use 'L-BFGS-B' for local minimization. The key advantage is the annealing schedule allows escaping local optima that trap DE.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: Maximize sum of radii by finding better global optima in the 52-dimensional position space
tried_paradigms.cautions: Use appropriate bounds [0.02, 0.98] for each coordinate. Set maxiter high enough (1000+) but within timeout. The radius computation must handle edge cases where circles are very close.

tried_paradigms.idea: Use greedy sequential placement with local refinement - fundamentally different construction paradigm
tried_paradigms.description: Instead of optimizing all 26 positions simultaneously, place circles one at a time greedily. For each new circle, find the position that maximizes its radius given all previously placed circles. Use a grid search or random sampling to find the best position for each new circle. After all circles are placed, optionally apply a local optimization to fine-tune. This constructive approach builds a good solution incrementally and avoids the high-dimensional optimization trap.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: Each placement maximizes the new circle's radius, then overall sum through refinement
tried_paradigms.cautions: The order of placement matters - consider placing larger circles first or using a strategic ordering. Grid resolution affects both quality and speed. Must ensure final configuration is valid.

tried_paradigms.idea: Use shapely geometric operations for precise maximum radius computation
tried_paradigms.description: Leverage shapely library's geometric primitives to compute maximum inscribed circles exactly. For each center position, create the feasible region by subtracting all other circles (as buffers) from the unit square, then find the maximum radius as the distance to nearest boundary. This gives exact radii rather than iterative approximations. Combine with any position optimizer (even the existing DE) but with more accurate objective function evaluation. The geometric approach handles complex boundary interactions correctly.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: More accurate radius computation leads to better optimization landscape and higher achievable sums
tried_paradigms.cautions: shapely may not be available - check imports. Buffer operations have floating point precision limits. The feasible region computation must handle degenerate cases where circles nearly touch.

tried_paradigms.idea: Use scipy.optimize.shgo (Simplicial Homology Global Optimization) for joint position-radius optimization
tried_paradigms.description: SHGO is specifically designed for constrained global optimization and hasn't been tried. Optimize all 78 variables (26×2 positions + 26 radii) jointly. Define nonlinear constraints for: (1) boundary constraints: r_i <= x_i, r_i <= y_i, r_i <= 1-x_i, r_i <= 1-y_i; (2) non-overlap constraints: r_i + r_j <= sqrt((x_i-x_j)^2 + (y_i-y_j)^2). SHGO uses simplicial homology to find global minima in constrained spaces, which is more appropriate than differential evolution for this constraint-heavy problem. Use the 'sobol' sampling method for better coverage.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: Maximize sum of all 26 radii directly as the objective function
tried_paradigms.cautions: Non-overlap constraints are nonlinear inequality constraints. Use epsilon=1e-6 tolerance in constraints. Set n=200 or higher for adequate sampling. The iters parameter controls refinement iterations.

tried_paradigms.idea: Use scipy.spatial.Voronoi diagram to compute optimal radii from positions
tried_paradigms.description: Instead of iterative radius computation, use Voronoi tessellation. For fixed positions, compute the Voronoi diagram. Each cell's boundaries represent the 'natural' region for that circle. The minimum distance from each site to its Voronoi cell edges gives an upper bound on radius. Clip Voronoi cells to the unit square boundaries. This gives mathematically optimal radii for the given positions without iterative approximation. Then use differential_evolution for position optimization with Voronoi-based radius computation in the objective function.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: Position optimization with Voronoi-computed radii as the true objective
tried_paradigms.cautions: Handle degenerate Voronoi cells (when points are collinear). Use scipy.spatial.Voronoi with qhull_options='Qbb Qc Qz' for robustness. Need to clip Voronoi cells to unit square boundaries. Points near edges need special handling.

tried_paradigms.idea: Use scipy.optimize.linprog for optimal radius computation given positions
tried_paradigms.description: For fixed positions, the radius maximization is actually a linear program: maximize sum(r_i) subject to r_i + r_j <= d_ij (pairwise distance constraints) and r_i <= min(x_i, y_i, 1-x_i, 1-y_i) (boundary constraints). All constraints are linear in radii! Use scipy.optimize.linprog with the 'highs' method to solve this LP exactly for any given positions. This finds the globally optimal radii for those positions, not just an approximation. Wrap this in differential_evolution for position optimization.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: Position optimization with LP-computed optimal radii
tried_paradigms.cautions: Build the constraint matrix carefully: for n=26 circles, there are C(26,2)=325 pairwise constraints plus 26 boundary constraints per circle (actually 4 each, but we can use the minimum). The objective vector is all ones. Use bounds=(0, None) for radii. The 'highs' method is the fastest LP solver in scipy.
